title: As If You Were Present
subtitle: On meditating the Passion of the Lord, in general

stillness: Let the door of the senses close.
Augustine says: if the soul has something to delight it without, it remains without delight within.
So refuse, for a little while, every other consolation.
gospel: Far be it from me to glory,
save in the cross of my Lord Jesus Christ;
through whom the world is crucified to me,
If we suffer together with Him,
we shall also be glorified together with Him.
behold: It will be needful, sometimes, to reckon yourself present in your very thought,
as though you had then and there been present when He suffered.
Carry yourself so in speaking, in living, in grieving,
as if you beheld your Lord before your own eyes, suffering.
Make yourself present, with tenderness, with care, and without haste,
to all and to each of the things that came to pass in the Lord's Passion.
Direct there your whole mind's gaze, with the waking eyes of the heart,
and forgetting all other cares, hold there, unwavering.
For the Lord Himself will then be present to you in spirit,
as present as you reckon Him to be.
consider: The Passion of the Lord ought to be recalled by a Christian at least seven times a day.
Bernard says: the daily reading of a Christian should be the remembrance of the Lord's Passion;
for nothing so kindles the human heart as the Passion of the Savior, often and earnestly thought upon.
As the scars and wounds remain ever in His body, and are not blotted out,
so in our heart, by remembrance, must He be held, and never blotted out.
If with the whole gaze of the mind these mysteries were perceived,
they would lead the one meditating into an utterly new state.
For to one who searches them out from the depth of the heart and the marrow of the inward parts,
many unlooked-for passages would arise:
a new compassion, a new love, new consolations,
a certain new state of sweetness, that seems a foretaste and a sharing of glory.
There was a devout nun so moved toward the Passion of Christ,
that she could never look upon the image of the Crucified,
but that, growing faint, she sank to the ground as though wholly carried out of herself.
Carnal consolation does not befit this remembrance.
The cross of Christ is set against pleasure, and pleasure against the cross.
Flesh and spirit cannot be delighted at once; they war one against the other.
Refuse to be consoled in other things, if you would be delighted in the memory of God.
The manna failed the children of Israel once they ate of the fruits of the land:
so the sweetness of Jesus fails the cloistered soul, when it begins to feed on earthly consolations.
Bitter and hard things must grow sweet to you,
if in the sorrows and Passion of Christ you long to find the sweet banquet of your soul.
Have compassion, then, on our Savior Jesus,
holding in your heart the memory of His Passion and His wounds,
most surely trusting that you will be the companion of His consolation in time to come,
if in the present you are found the companion of His tribulation.
And not in heart only, but in body also, must we suffer with Christ.
prayer: pour into me, a sinner, I beseech You, the fullness of Your love;
that I may desire nothing earthly or carnal,
but love You alone above all things,
so that my soul may utterly refuse to be consoled,
save in You, my most sweet God.
Write with Your finger upon the tablets of my heart
the memory of all that You bore for me,
that I may have it ever before my eyes;
and grant that it may grow sweet to me not only to ponder these things,
but also, if need be, to bear them in my small measure;
and not only to serve You with all my strength,
but even, for Your sake, to suffer reproach,
or to be condemned to the basest death.

latinJewel.latin: Necessarium enim erit, ut aliquando ita cogites te praesentem cogitatione tua, ac si tunc temporis ibi praesens fuisses quando passus fuit.
latinJewel.english: For it will be needful that you sometimes so reckon yourself present in your thought, as though you had then and there been present when He suffered.
